title: Acceptance Remarks by Chair Jerome H. Powell at the American Society for Public Administration Annual Conference: Paul A. Volcker Public Integrity Award Ceremony
summary: Fed Chair Jerome Powell delivered acceptance remarks via pre-recorded video on March 21, 2026, receiving the Paul A. Volcker Public Integrity Award from the American Society for Public Administration. The speech is entirely a historical tribute to Paul Volcker's legacy as Fed Chairman from 1979-1987, focusing on his commitment to public service integrity, his role in defeating double-digit inflation in the early 1980s, and the principles of central bank independence. The speech contains no forward guidance, policy signals, economic projections, or commentary on current monetary policy or market conditions.
key_points: Powell accepted the Paul A. Volcker Public Integrity Award via pre-recorded video on March 21, 2026.
The speech focuses exclusively on Paul Volcker's legacy as Fed Chairman (1979-1987) and his role in conquering double-digit inflation.
Powell emphasizes that Volcker demonstrated that central bank independence and integrity are inseparable principles of effective public service.
No commentary is provided on current monetary policy, interest rate path, inflation outlook, or economic conditions.
No forward guidance on rates, balance sheet operations, or any Fed policy tools is contained in this remarks.
The speech is ceremonial and backward-looking, serving as a tribute to Volcker's contributions rather than a policy communication.
why_it_matters: This speech has no market relevance. It is a ceremonial award acceptance speech that contains no policy signals, economic data, or forward guidance of any kind. Market participants should not interpret this as containing any implied Fed views on monetary policy. The speech is historically focused on honoring Paul Volcker's legacy and principles of public service integrity.

human_review.notes: This source is not relevant to market structure or macro catalysts. It contains no economic analysis, policy signals, or market-moving content. The speech is purely a ceremonial tribute to Paul Volcker's legacy. While the speaker is the Fed Chair, the content has no bearing on financial markets or monetary policy expectations. Recommend excluding from research archive unless the research purpose specifically includes Fed communication archive (in which case it should be filed separately from policy-relevant communications).
